title: Configuration Changes & RROs
description: Config change triggers, state preservation strategies, resource qualifiers, and Runtime Resource Overlays

references.title: Android Developers — Change app resources at runtime

lesson:
What Triggers a Configuration Change?

A configuration change occurs whenever a system property that affects resource resolution changes at runtime. The system destroys and recreates the Activity so it can reload resources matching the new configuration.

TriggerConfig FlagCommon Scenario
Screen rotationorientationUser rotates phone
Dark mode toggleuiModeSystem or quick-tile toggle
Locale changelocaleUser changes language in Settings
Font scalefontScaleAccessibility → Display size
Screen size / densityscreenSize, densityFoldable fold/unfold, display mode
Keyboard availabilitykeyboard, keyboardHiddenPhysical keyboard attached/detached
Smallest screen widthsmallestScreenSizeMulti-window resize

Default Behavior: Destroy + Recreate

When a config change fires, the system calls onSaveInstanceState() → onDestroy() → onCreate() with the saved Bundle. This is intentional — the Activity must reload resources (layouts, strings, drawables) that may differ for the new configuration.

Config change fires (e.g., rotation) │ ├── onPause() ├── onSaveInstanceState(outState) ← save transient UI state ├── onStop() ├── onDestroy() ← old Activity instance dies │ ├── onCreate(savedInstanceState) ← NEW instance, new resources loaded ├── onStart() └── onResume() ViewModel survives — same instance, new Activity

android:configChanges — Handle It Yourself

Declaring android:configChanges in the manifest tells the system not to destroy your Activity for those specific changes. Instead, onConfigurationChanged() is called.

<activity android:name=".VideoPlayerActivity" android:configChanges="orientation|screenSize|smallestScreenSize" />
// Called instead of destroy/recreate override fun onConfigurationChanged(newConfig: Configuration) { super.onConfigurationChanged(newConfig) // Manually handle the change if (newConfig.orientation == Configuration.ORIENTATION_LANDSCAPE) { enterFullscreen() } }
When to use this (rarely):
- Video/media players: pausing playback on rotation is unacceptable
- Maps: rebuilding the map state is expensive
- WebViews: reloading a page loses user context

When NOT to use this:
- Normal UI apps — you lose automatic resource reloading (dark mode drawables, landscape layouts, locale strings). You would need to manually reload every resource, which is error-prone and defeats the purpose of the resource system.

State Preservation Matrix

Different state-saving mechanisms survive different destruction scenarios. This is the most critical table to memorize:

MechanismSurvives Config ChangeSurvives Process DeathLimitUse For
ViewModelYESNOHeap memoryScreen state, loaded data, ongoing operations
onSaveInstanceStateYESYES~500KB (Bundle transaction limit)Small transient UI state: scroll position, selection, text input
SavedStateHandleYESYES~500KB (uses SavedState under the hood)ViewModel state that must survive process death
rememberSaveableYESYES~500KB (backed by SavedState)Compose equivalent of onSaveInstanceState
remember { }NO (recomposition only)NOHeap memoryDerived/computed values within a composition
Room / DataStoreYES (persistent)YES (persistent)Disk spaceUser data, preferences, cached responses
The SavedState 500KB Limit: The Bundle is serialized through Binder, which has a 1MB transaction buffer shared across ALL operations. In practice, keep your saved state well under 500KB. Store IDs or keys, not full objects — reload the data from ViewModel or repository after restoration.
// SavedStateHandle in ViewModel — survives BOTH config change AND process death class SearchViewModel( private val savedStateHandle: SavedStateHandle ) : ViewModel() { // Automatically saved/restored across process death val query = savedStateHandle.getStateFlow("query", "") fun onQueryChanged(newQuery: String) { savedStateHandle["query"] = newQuery } }
// Compose: rememberSaveable vs remember @Composable fun SearchBar() { // SURVIVES config change + process death var query by rememberSaveable { mutableStateOf("") } // LOST on config change — only survives recomposition var expanded by remember { mutableStateOf(false) } }

Resource Qualifiers & Directory Structure

Android resolves resources by matching directory qualifiers to the current configuration. Qualifiers are appended to the resource directory name with a dash:

QualifierExample DirectoryMatches When
Night modevalues-night/Dark theme active
Orientationlayout-land/Landscape orientation
Smallest widthlayout-sw600dp/Tablet (shortest edge >= 600dp)
API levelvalues-v31/API 31+ (Android 12+)
Localevalues-es/, values-ja/Spanish, Japanese
Densitydrawable-xxhdpi/~480dpi screens
res/ ├── layout/ │ └── activity_main.xml ← default (portrait, phone) ├── layout-land/ │ └── activity_main.xml ← landscape ├── layout-sw600dp/ │ └── activity_main.xml ← tablet ├── layout-sw600dp-land/ │ └── activity_main.xml ← tablet landscape ├── values/ │ ├── strings.xml ← default (English) │ ├── colors.xml ← light theme colors │ └── themes.xml ├── values-night/ │ └── colors.xml ← dark theme colors ├── values-es/ │ └── strings.xml ← Spanish strings ├── values-v31/ │ └── themes.xml ← Material You dynamic colors ├── drawable-mdpi/ ← ~160dpi ├── drawable-hdpi/ ← ~240dpi ├── drawable-xhdpi/ ← ~320dpi ├── drawable-xxhdpi/ ← ~480dpi └── drawable-xxxhdpi/ ← ~640dpi

When multiple qualifiers match, Android uses a best-match algorithm that eliminates non-matching directories one qualifier at a time, in precedence order (MCC → language → layout direction → smallest width → screen size → orientation → UI mode → night → density → ...). The most specific surviving match wins.

Runtime Resource Overlays (RROs)

RROs let you override any app's resources at runtime without modifying its APK. They are the mechanism behind OEM theming, Material You dynamic colors, and carrier customizations.

How RROs Work

Target App (com.example.app) ├── res/values/colors.xml │ └── colorPrimary = #6200EE ← original │ Overlay APK (com.example.overlay) ├── res/values/colors.xml │ └── colorPrimary = #FF5722 ← override! ├── AndroidManifest.xml │ └── <overlay targetPackage="com.example.app" .../> │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ idmap2: builds mapping at install time │ │ target resource ID → overlay resource │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ At runtime: app reads colorPrimary → gets #FF5722 (overlay wins)

Creating an RRO: AndroidManifest.xml

<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android" package="com.example.myoverlay"> <overlay android:targetPackage="com.android.systemui" android:targetName="SystemUIOverlay" android:isStatic="false" android:priority="1" android:category="android.theme" /> <application android:hasCode="false" /> </manifest>

Key attributes:

AttributePurpose
targetPackagePackage name of the app whose resources to override
targetNameName of the overlayable group (API 29+) — limits which resources can be overlaid
isStatictrue = always enabled, cannot be toggled. false = can be enabled/disabled at runtime
priorityHigher priority overlays win when multiple target the same resource

Static vs Dynamic Overlays

Static OverlayDynamic Overlay
Enabled byAlways on at bootMust be explicitly enabled
Toggle at runtimeNoYes — cmd overlay enable/disable
Use caseOEM branding, carrier configTheming, user preferences
RequiresSigned with platform key or pre-installedAppropriate permissions

Overlayable Resources (API 29+)

Starting in Android 10, target apps declare which resources can be overlaid using <overlayable>:

<!-- In the TARGET app's res/values/overlayable.xml --> <resources> <overlayable name="SystemUIOverlay"> <policy type="public"> <item type="color" name="colorPrimary" /> <item type="dimen" name="status_bar_height" /> <item type="bool" name="config_enableLockScreen" /> </policy> </overlayable> </resources>

Fabricated Overlays (API 31+ / Android 12)

Fabricated overlays let the system create overlay resources in memory without an APK. This is how Material You generates dynamic color themes from the user's wallpaper:

// System-only API — used internally by ThemeOverlayController val overlay = FabricatedOverlay.Builder("com.android.theme.color", "wallpaper") .setTargetPackage("android") .setResourceValue("color/system_accent1_100", TypedValue.TYPE_INT_COLOR_ARGB8, extractedColor) .build() OverlayManager.commit(OverlayManagerTransaction.newInstance().registerFabricatedOverlay(overlay))
Material You flow: wallpaper change → color extraction → fabricated overlay created → system_accent1/2/3, system_neutral1/2 color palettes updated → all apps using ?attr/colorPrimary (Material3) automatically pick up new colors without any app changes.

Shell Commands for RRO Debugging

# List all overlays and their state (enabled/disabled) adb shell cmd overlay list # Enable/disable a specific overlay adb shell cmd overlay enable com.example.myoverlay adb shell cmd overlay disable com.example.myoverlay # Dump detailed overlay info adb shell cmd overlay dump # Inspect idmap2 mappings (target → overlay resource resolution) adb shell idmap2 dump --idmap-path /data/resource-cache/com.example.myoverlay@overlay.apk@idmap # List all idmap files adb shell ls /data/resource-cache/

How OEMs Use RROs

RROs are the primary customization mechanism for OEMs. They allow heavy theming without forking AOSP source code:

OEMWhat They OverlayExamples
Samsung OneUISystemUI, Settings, frameworkRounded corner radii, notification shade colors, default ringtones, status bar height, Quick Settings tile layout
Google PixelSystem, SystemUI, LauncherMaterial You dynamic colors (fabricated overlays), Pixel-specific feature flags, default wallpapers
CarriersSettings, TelephonyProviderAPN configs, VoLTE/VoWiFi toggles, carrier branding strings
Xiaomi MIUI / HyperOSFramework, SystemUI, core appsControl center layout, icon shapes, animation durations
# On a Pixel device, you can see the overlay stack: adb shell cmd overlay list android # Output shows fabricated overlays for Material You colors: # [x] com.android.theme.color.custom.overlay (enabled, fabricated) # [x] com.google.android.overlay.gmsconfig (enabled, static)

This is why the same AOSP-based Android looks completely different on a Samsung vs. Pixel vs. Xiaomi device — it's largely RROs, not source code forks.

questions.title: Config Change Default Behavior
questions.description: What happens to an Activity by default when the user rotates the device?
options: onConfigurationChanged() is called and the Activity continues running
The Activity is destroyed and a new instance is created with savedInstanceState
The Activity is paused but not destroyed
Only the Fragment is recreated, the Activity stays alive
questions.explanation: By default, a configuration change (like rotation) causes the system to call onSaveInstanceState(), then fully destroy the Activity (onDestroy()), and create a brand new instance (onCreate()) with the saved Bundle. This ensures the Activity loads resources matching the new configuration — for example, a landscape-specific layout from layout-land/.
hints: The system needs to reload resources that may differ for the new configuration
Think about what happens to layout files when going portrait → landscape

questions.title: State Preservation — ViewModel vs SavedStateHandle
questions.description: A user is filling out a multi-step form. They get a phone call, the app goes to background, and the system kills the process to reclaim memory. When the user returns, which state-saving mechanisms still have their data?
options: ViewModel only — it survives everything
onSaveInstanceState and SavedStateHandle — ViewModel data is lost on process death
All state is lost — the user must start over
Room/DataStore only — all in-memory state is lost
questions.explanation: ViewModel survives configuration changes (rotation, dark mode) but does NOT survive process death. onSaveInstanceState and SavedStateHandle survive BOTH because their data is serialized into a Bundle held by the system. For critical form data, use SavedStateHandle in your ViewModel to get the best of both: ViewModel convenience + process death survival. For truly persistent data, write to Room or DataStore.
hints: Process death means the entire process is killed — all in-memory objects are gone
What mechanism serializes data for the system to hold outside the process?

questions.title: android:configChanges Side Effects
questions.description: A developer adds this to their manifest. What are the consequences?
options: The Activity handles all config changes efficiently — this is best practice

The Activity is never recreated for these changes, but it also never reloads resources automatically — dark mode drawables, landscape layouts, locale strings, and scaled text must ALL be handled manually in onConfigurationChanged()
The Activity ignores these config changes entirely — nothing happens
Only orientation is handled, the other flags are ignored
questions.explanation: By declaring all these config changes, the Activity opts out of automatic recreation for rotation, dark mode, locale, and font scale changes. The Activity stays alive and onConfigurationChanged() is called instead. However, resources are not automatically reloaded. If you have layout-land/, values-night/, values-es/, or values-sw600dp/ directories, those alternative resources will NOT be loaded. You must manually update every resource — which is why this approach is almost never appropriate for normal UI apps.
hints: Think about what the system normally does during destroy/recreate that you're now skipping
Consider dark mode drawables, landscape layouts, and translated strings

questions.title: SavedStateHandle in ViewModel
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create a ViewModel that preserves the search query across both configuration changes AND process death.

blanks.hint: The Jetpack class that gives ViewModel access to SavedState
Returns a StateFlow that automatically updates when the saved value changes

questions.explanation: SavedStateHandle bridges the gap between ViewModel (survives config changes but not process death) and SavedState (survives both). By using getStateFlow(), you get a reactive StateFlow that automatically reflects changes. The data is serialized to a Bundle under the hood, so it survives process death — but keep values small (under 500KB total).
hints: This class is injected into ViewModels automatically by Hilt or the default ViewModelProvider
The method returns a StateFlow backed by the saved state

questions.title: RRO Overlay Mechanics
questions.description: An OEM wants to change the default accent color of SystemUI without modifying any AOSP source code. What is the correct approach?
options: Fork the SystemUI source and change the color constant
Create a Runtime Resource Overlay APK that targets com.android.systemui and overrides the color resource, then pre-install it as a static overlay
Use adb shell settings put to change the color at runtime
Modify the framework's attrs.xml to change the default theme color
questions.explanation: RROs are specifically designed for this use case. The OEM creates a small overlay APK containing only the replacement color resource, with a manifest declaring targetPackage="com.android.systemui" and isStatic="true". At build time, the overlay is pre-installed in the system partition. At boot, idmap2 creates a mapping from SystemUI's original color resource ID to the overlay's replacement. SystemUI loads the OEM's color without any source code changes.
hints: The goal is to override resources WITHOUT modifying the original APK
Think about what mechanism Android provides for resource-level customization

questions.title: Static vs Dynamic Overlays
questions.description: What is the key difference between a static and dynamic RRO?
options: Static overlays are faster, dynamic overlays are slower
Static overlays are always enabled and cannot be toggled at runtime; dynamic overlays can be enabled/disabled via cmd overlay or OverlayManager API
Static overlays work on all API levels, dynamic overlays require API 31+
Static overlays can only change colors, dynamic overlays can change layouts
questions.explanation: A static overlay (android:isStatic="true") is always active from boot — used for OEM branding that should never change. A dynamic overlay (android:isStatic="false") can be toggled at runtime using cmd overlay enable/disable or the OverlayManager system service. Dynamic overlays are used for user-selectable themes, toggling features, or Material You color application.
hints: Think about OEM branding (should always be on) vs. user-selected themes (togglable)

questions.title: Fabricated Overlays and Material You
questions.description: Explain how Material You (dynamic color theming) works under the hood using fabricated overlays on Android 12+.
questions.answer: When the user changes their wallpaper on Android 12+, the system extracts dominant colors using a color-extraction algorithm. ThemeOverlayController then creates fabricated overlays — overlay resources generated in memory without needing an APK on disk. These overlays target the 'android' framework package and override the system_accent1/2/3 and system_neutral1/2 color palettes. Since fabricated overlays use the standard RRO idmap2 machinery, any app that references Material3 theme attributes (like ?attr/colorPrimary) automatically picks up the new colors without any code changes or app updates.
questions.explanation: Fabricated overlays (introduced in API 31) are the key innovation that makes Material You possible at scale. Previously, theme overlays required pre-built APKs. Fabricated overlays are created entirely in memory by the system server, making it possible to generate an unlimited number of color themes from any wallpaper. The FabricatedOverlay.Builder API allows setting individual resource values programmatically, and the overlay is applied through the same OverlayManager infrastructure as traditional RROs.
hints: Think about how the system can create unique color themes for every wallpaper without pre-building APKs
Material You colors are applied to ALL apps — what system mechanism can override resources across all packages?

questions.title: State Lost on Process Death
questions.description: Users report that the selected tab is lost when they switch to another app and come back. The ViewModel survives rotation fine but not process death. Fix it.

checks.message: Use SavedStateHandle to survive process death
Use getStateFlow() or getLiveData() from SavedStateHandle for reactive state

questions.explanation: The original code uses a plain MutableStateFlow which lives in ViewModel memory — it survives config changes but is destroyed with the process. By switching to SavedStateHandle.getStateFlow(), the selected tab index is automatically serialized into a Bundle. When the system kills and restores the process, the Bundle is provided back to the new ViewModel instance, and the tab selection is restored. This is the standard pattern for any small UI state that must survive process death.
hints: ViewModel survives rotation but NOT process death — what addition makes it survive both?
The fix requires adding a constructor parameter
